provenance.title: Eigenvector rotation precedes eigenvalue-based early-warning signals: a TVP-Kalman approach to detecting critical transitions
affiliations: Laboratory of Applied Economics and Management Sciences, Department of Agribusiness, Faculty of Economics and Management, University of Dschang, Cameroon
provenance.abstract: Classical EWS rely on the dominant eigenvalue (variance, AR(1)), with O(dtheta^2) sensitivity and limited lead time. This paper introduces a complementary EWS based on eigenvector rotation, measured by elasticity beta(t)=dlog(y)/dlog(x) via a TVP-Kalman filter, with O(dtheta) sensitivity. On NASA AIRS data (3 regions) and 6 simulated tipping systems, beta is orthogonal to AR(1) and precedes it by 14-24 months (obs.) and 39-153 timesteps (sim.).
key_claims: Introduces a new early-warning-signal (EWS) class based on eigenvector rotation, measured by a dimensionless time-varying elasticity beta(t) = dlog(y)/dlog(x) estimated via a TVP-Kalman filter in log-log space
beta has O(delta_theta) sensitivity to bifurcation-parameter perturbations versus O(delta_theta^2) for classical eigenvalue-based EWS (AR(1), variance), theoretically implying longer lead time

On 24 years of monthly NASA AIRS data across three climate regions (Arctic, Tropics, Indian Monsoon), beta is empirically orthogonal to AR(1) (Pearson r ~ 0, n.s.) in all regions, and systematically precedes AR(1) by 14-24 months in 5 of 6 region-signal pairs

On six simulated systems with known tipping points, beta leads AR(1) by 39-153 timesteps specifically when the transition involves coupling degradation (e.g. Stommel AMOC model, critical slowing down), but does not detect a univariate fold bifurcation where coupling itself is unchanged

The Tropics region's beta (~0.49) closely matches the theoretical Clausius-Clapeyron scaling exponent (~0.5), while the Indian Monsoon shows a recent deceleration (beta'' < 0) interpreted as a potential early signal of monsoon circulation weakening

architecture.primary_variant: TVP-Kalman elasticity filter (4-state, order-3 Taylor expansion) with RTS backward smoothing, benchmarked against classical rolling-window EWS (AR(1), variance, permutation entropy, mutual information) via lead-lag cross-correlation analysis

modules.name: TVP-Kalman forward filter
modules.input_shape: scalar time series pair (log T_k, log q_k), k=1..284 (or simulation length)
modules.output_shape: filtered state estimate x_k|k = [beta_k, beta'_k, beta''_k, beta'''_k]^T and covariance P_k|k, for each k

parameters.transition_matrix_F: order-3 Taylor expansion transition matrix (exact numeric entries not given in paper -- see implementation_assumptions)

modules.notes: Observation equation log(q_k) = beta_k * log(T_k) + eps_k treats log(T_k) as a time-varying regressor (i.e. observation matrix H_k = [log(T_k), 0, 0, 0]), a standard time-varying-parameter (TVP) regression-via-Kalman-filter formulation (Harvey-style), though the paper does not spell out the generic Kalman recursion equations themselves.

modules.name: RTS backward smoother
modules.input_shape: forward-filtered states/covariances x_k|k, P_k|k for k=1..N and predicted x_k|k-1, P_k|k-1
modules.output_shape: smoothed state estimates x_k|N = [beta_k, beta'_k, beta''_k, beta'''_k]^T for each k
modules.notes: Mentioned by name only ("Kalman filter forward pass is followed by RTS backward smoothing"); standard RTS recursion equations assumed, not given explicitly in the paper.

modules.name: Classical rolling-window EWS computation
modules.input_shape: scalar time series x_t (T or q), rolling windows of 24 or 36 months
modules.output_shape: time series of AR(1), variance, permutation entropy, and mutual-information values
modules.notes: AR(1) computed via linear regression of x_t on x_{t-1} within each rolling window; variance is the window sample variance; permutation entropy and mutual-information estimators are named but their exact implementations (e.g. specific kNN/MI estimator such as Kraskov) are not specified.

modules.name: Lead-lag cross-correlation analysis
modules.input_shape: pair of aligned time series (beta or its derivatives, and a classical EWS signal)
modules.output_shape: optimal lag (months) and lead/lag direction per region-signal pair (Table 2), plus lead-time-to-tipping-point per simulated system (Table 3)
modules.notes: For observational data, optimal lag is found via cross-correlation between beta (and its derivatives beta', beta'', beta''') and each classical EWS signal. For simulations, lead time is defined as the interval between each EWS's first significant deviation (|z|>2) and the known tipping point.

modules.name: Simulated tipping-point system generators
modules.input_shape: None (simulated from specified/partially-specified ODEs or maps)
modules.output_shape: simulated (x_t, y_t) or x_t time series with a known tipping-point time

systems: Fold bifurcation: dx/dt = r(t) - x^2 + eta, r -> 0 at t=200
Beta step change: y = beta(t)*x + eps, beta: 0.8 -> 0.2 at t=200
Beta linear decay: beta decays from 1.0 to 0.2 (coupling degradation)
Stommel AMOC model: 2-box model with freshwater forcing
Critical slowing down: dx/dt = -lambda(t)*x + eta, lambda -> 0

modules.notes: Only qualitative equations/parameter endpoints are given for each of the six systems; noise magnitudes (eta), exact forcing schedules (e.g. the Stommel AMOC freshwater-forcing trajectory), and simulation length/dt are not fully specified, making exact numeric reproduction of Table 3's lead times unlikely without additional assumptions.

connections.from: raw AIRS T, q series (or simulated x, y)

connections.tensor_name: forward state/covariance sequence (x_k|k, P_k|k, x_k|k-1, P_k|k-1)

connections.from: raw AIRS T, q series (or simulated x, y)

connections.tensor_name: AR(1), variance, permutation entropy, mutual information time series

differences.data: 284 monthly observations per region, 3 regions (Arctic, Tropics, Indian Monsoon)
differences.purpose: test orthogonality and lead-lag of beta vs classical EWS on real climate data (Section 3.1-3.3)

differences.data: 6 synthetic dynamical systems with known bifurcation times
differences.purpose: validate that beta leads AR(1) specifically when coupling degrades (Section 3.4)

mathematical_spec.name: TVP-Kalman state transition equation
hyperparameters: F: order-3 Taylor expansion transition matrix, dt=1/12 year
undefined_symbols: F (exact numeric entries not given in the paper)

mathematical_spec.name: Sensitivity-order theoretical prediction (eigenvector vs eigenvalue)
undefined_symbols: delta_theta (distance to bifurcation in parameter space)

mathematical_spec.name: AR(1) lag-1 autocorrelation (rolling window)
mathematical_spec.latex: x_t = \text{AR}(1) \cdot x_{t-1} + \text{const} + e_t \quad \text{(estimated via linear regression within a 24-month rolling window)}

tensor_semantics.name: Raw AIRS surface skin temperature series T
tensor_semantics.module: TVP-Kalman forward filter / Classical rolling-window EWS computation

tensor_semantics.name: Raw AIRS surface specific/water-vapor humidity series q
tensor_semantics.module: TVP-Kalman forward filter / Classical rolling-window EWS computation

tensor_semantics.name: State covariance matrix P_k
tensor_semantics.module: TVP-Kalman forward filter / RTS backward smoother

tensor_semantics.name: Classical EWS time series (AR(1), variance, permutation entropy, mutual information)
tensor_semantics.shape_notation: [284 - window_length + 1] per EWS metric, per region, per variable

tensor_semantics.name: Region-level summary statistics (Table 1)
tensor_semantics.shape_notation: scalars per region: N, |beta| mean, sigma_beta, r(beta,AR1_T), r(beta,MI), transitions count

training_pipeline.curriculum: Not applicable: this paper uses a fixed-hyperparameter linear Kalman filter (R, Q set a priori, Section 2.2), not a trained/optimized model in the machine-learning sense. There is no gradient-based training loop, loss minimisation, or learned parameter fitting -- R and Q are specified directly as filter design choices.

datasets.name: NASA AIRS (Atmospheric Infrared Sounder) monthly data, Arctic region (65-90N)
datasets.split: single time series, N=284 months (Sept 2002 - Apr 2026)

datasets.name: NASA AIRS monthly data, Tropics region (10S-10N)
NASA AIRS monthly data, Indian Monsoon region (60E-100E, 5N-25N)
Six simulated tipping-point systems (fold bifurcation, beta step change, beta linear decay, logistic map, Stommel AMOC, critical slowing down)
datasets.split: synthetic, per-system length varies (~250-400 timesteps based on Figure 3 axes)

metrics: Pearson correlation r(beta, AR(1)_T) per region
Pearson correlation r(beta, MI) per region
mean absolute elasticity |beta| and its standard deviation sigma_beta per region
number of beta regime transitions (beta''=0 crossings) per region
optimal cross-correlation lag (months) between beta/derivatives and each classical EWS signal, per region
lead time (timesteps) between first significant (|z|>2) EWS deviation and known tipping point, per simulated system

reported_results.metric: beta lead time over AR(1) (Stommel AMOC simulation)
beta lead time over AR(1) (critical slowing down simulation)
beta lead-lag range vs classical EWS (observational)

baselines: Precomputed DCC-GARCH correlation, Transfer Entropy, and Convergent Cross Mapping results referenced from prior work on the same dataset (not recomputed in this paper)
evaluation_protocol.compute: Not specified in the paper. Given the small dataset size (284 monthly observations per region) and simple linear Kalman filter / classical rolling-window statistics, computation is expected to be lightweight and CPU-only (no GPU, no deep learning framework required).
special_conditions: AR(1) and variance use a 24-month rolling window; permutation entropy and mutual information use a 36-month rolling window -- the two EWS families are computed over different window lengths
beta significance/orthogonality assessed via Pearson correlation with reported p-values (p>0.05 taken as non-significant, i.e. orthogonal)
Lead-time analysis in simulations uses a |z|>2 significance threshold for first detection of each EWS
Six simulated systems are used purely for controlled validation of the lead-time mechanism, not fit to any real data

implementation_assumptions.assumption: The order-3 Taylor-expansion state-transition matrix F is not given numerically in the paper. It is assumed to be the standard discretised kinematic/Taylor chain matrix for a 4-state (value, 1st, 2nd, 3rd derivative) local polynomial trend model: F = [[1, dt, dt^2/2, dt^3/6], [0, 1, dt, dt^2/2], [0, 0, 1, dt], [0, 0, 0, 1]], with dt = 1/12 year, consistent with x_k = [beta_k, beta'_k, beta''_k, beta'''_k]^T.
implementation_assumptions.basis: standard state-space/Kalman-filter convention for polynomial (Taylor-series) trend models
alternatives: a discrete-time ARIMA-style companion-form transition matrix
a differently-scaled Taylor matrix (e.g. without the dt^2/2, dt^3/6 factorial scaling)

implementation_assumptions.assumption: The observation model treats log(T_k) as a time-varying regressor (observation matrix H_k = [log(T_k), 0, 0, 0]) so that the Kalman filter estimates a genuinely time-varying elasticity beta_k, consistent with standard time-varying-parameter (TVP) regression via Kalman filter (Harvey-style state-space regression).
implementation_assumptions.basis: standard TVP-regression-via-Kalman-filter convention in econometrics/time-series literature
alternatives: a local-level model on beta alone with T,q entering only through a pre-computed log-ratio series

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Permutation entropy and mutual-information estimators use standard, commonly-available implementations (e.g. Bandt-Pompe ordinal-pattern permutation entropy; a k-nearest-neighbor-based mutual information estimator such as Kraskov-Stogbauer-Grassberger) since the paper names the methods but not the exact algorithm/library/parameters (beyond embedding dimension m=3 and window length).
implementation_assumptions.basis: field-standard estimator choices for the named methods
alternatives: a different ordinal-pattern permutation entropy normalisation

implementation_assumptions.assumption: The six simulated tipping-point systems' noise magnitudes (eta, epsilon), exact forcing schedules (especially the Stommel AMOC 2-box model's freshwater-forcing trajectory), simulation lengths, and time steps are not fully specified numerically in the paper (only qualitative equations and bifurcation-parameter endpoints are given), so exact reproduction of Table 3's specific lead-time values (in timesteps) is not guaranteed without further assumptions.
implementation_assumptions.basis: explicit gaps in the paper's Section 2.4 description
alternatives: parameter values may be recoverable from the author's code repository (https://github.com/GildasTiwangNg), which is referenced but not detailed in-text
implementation_assumptions.affects_section: architecture (Simulated tipping-point system generators), mathematical_spec (six simulated-system equations)

implementation_assumptions.assumption: The exact spatial-averaging procedure used to reduce NASA AIRS gridded data to a single regional time series per region (e.g. simple area-weighted mean over all grid cells within the stated lat/lon bounding box) is not specified in the paper beyond naming the three region bounding boxes.
implementation_assumptions.basis: standard practice for regional climate-index construction from gridded satellite products
alternatives: a specific sub-selection of grid cells
a different weighting scheme (e.g. cosine-latitude weighting)

ambiguities.description: The paper names the state-transition matrix F only as "the Taylor expansion transition matrix of order 3" without giving its numeric entries, and does not spell out the generic Kalman filter recursion (predict/update) equations or the RTS smoother recursion equations themselves -- these are assumed to be the textbook forms.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: F is the standard factorial-scaled Taylor/kinematic chain matrclass for a 4-state polynomial trend model with dt=1/12 year; standard Kalman/RTS recursions apply
alternatives: a differently normalised or truncated Taylor matrix

ambiguities.location: Section 2.4 and Figure 3 (six simulated tipping-point systems)
ambiguities.description: Noise magnitudes, exact forcing schedules (especially for the Stommel AMOC 2-box model), simulation lengths, and integration time steps are not given numerically for any of the six systems -- only qualitative governing equations and bifurcation-parameter start/end values are provided.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Reasonable illustrative parameter values (small additive Gaussian noise, linear or sigmoid-like forcing ramps) would need to be chosen to approximately reproduce Table 3's reported lead times, but exact numeric matches are not guaranteed
alternatives: the author's code repository may contain the exact parameter values used

ambiguities.location: Section 2.3 (Classical EWS computed) and Data and Code Availability
ambiguities.description: The exact algorithms/libraries used for permutation entropy and mutual information estimation, and the precise NASA AIRS Giovanni-portal query/spatial-averaging procedure used to build each region's T and q series, are not specified in the text beyond high-level parameter choices (window lengths, embedding dimension).
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Standard, commonly-used estimator implementations and a simple area-weighted regional average are assumed
